2606.28676.title: Predicting Metastatic Risk from Primary Tissue Architecture via Distance-Aware Spatial Modeling
2606.28676.paper_summary: Predicting the risk of distant metastasis from primary tumor tissue histology is a critical yet challenging task in computational pathology. Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) approaches can attend to subdomains in tumor regions that harbor features of metastatic cancer progression. However MIL models treat tissue patches as unordered bags, discarding the spatial layout that defines the metastatic potential. We propose that metastatic risk is inherently dictated by the geometric arrangement of the tumor microenvironment at the interface with tumor cells. Our model is designed to explicitly capture the spatial relationships between tumor cells, tumor associated fibroblasts and infiltrating lymphocytes. For this purpose, we propose Distance aware Tissue Modeling for Multiple Instance Learning(DTMf-MIL), a novel method that reinforces visual features with explicit spatial priors. By computing signed distance functions (SDF) relative to tissue phenotypes, our model learns to recognize structural signatures of metastatic risk. This geometric awareness translates directly to superior clinical performance as DTMf-MIL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods that ignore spatial layout on metastasis prediction from tissue in the primary tumor. We further validate our approach on public benchmarks, demonstrating that spatial awareness consistently improves diagnostic accuracy across diverse clinical tasks.
2606.28676.paper_summary_zh: 預測來自原發腫瘤組織組織學的遠端轉移風險是一項關鍵但具有挑戰性的任務，在計算病理學中尤為重要。多實例學習（MIL）方法可以關注腫瘤區域中包含轉移性癌症進展特徵的子領域。然後，MIL模型將組織補丁視為無序的集合，忽略了定義轉移潛力的空間佈局。我們提出，轉移風險本質上是由腫瘤微環境與腫瘤細胞界面的幾何排列所決定。 我們的模型旨在明確捕捉腫瘤細胞、腫瘤相關成纖維細胞和浸潤淋巴細胞之間的空間關係。為此，我們提出了距離感知組織建模的多實例學習（DTMf-MIL），這是一種新穎的方法，通過明確的空間先驗來加強視覺特徵。通過計算相對於組織表型的簽名距離函數（SDF），我們的模型學會識別轉移風險的結構特徵。這種幾何意識直接轉化為優越的臨床表現，因為DTMf-MIL在原發腫瘤組織的轉移預測中顯著超越了忽略空間佈局的最先進方法。我們進一步在公共基準上驗證我們的方法，證明空間意識在各種臨床任務中持續提高診斷準確性。
2606.28676.author: Sandesh Pokhrel et.al.
2606.28676.authors: Sandesh Pokhrel, Hamid Manoochehri, Bodong Zhang, Beatrice S Knudsen, Tolga Tasdizen
